div.head: 175. Memorandum From the Special Assistant to the President (Cutler) to the PresidentSource: Eisenhower Library, Project Clean Up Records, Panama Canal. Top Secret.

list.head: SUBJECT
list.item: National Security Aspects of the Existing Panama Canal

The Panama Canal Company is considering the expenditure of $19 million over 4 years, without Congressional appropriation, to improve the existing Canal and meet anticipated world shipping requirements for the next decade. Before undertaking this expenditure the Company has sought from Governor Adams assurance that the Administration has no present plan to reconstruct the present Panama Canal into a sea level canal or to construct through Panama or Nicaragua a new sea level canal.

item.p: In March, 1956, the Director of the Budget reported to Governor Adams,Document 129. after obtaining the views of interested departments and agencies, that the conversion to a sea level canal of the present Panama Canal would cost $3.6 billion; that the interested agencies assumed a rather negative attitude toward consideration of the project; that the Joint Chiefs of Staff considered the project of “lower priority than military projects and activities required for the security of the nation”; and that State thought a Presidential Commission to study the project was unnecessary because the defense considerations were paramount.
It is understood that the building of a new sea level canal through Nicaragua would be considerably more costly.

list.item: The Suez incident has occurred since March, 1956. Under the circumstances, do you wish any current expression from Defense–JCS as to the military importance and priority of a project to provide a sea level canal through Panama or Nicaragua?A handwritten note on the source text indicated that Cutler had discussed this subject with Eisenhower at Newport, Rhode Island, on September 16. For Cutler’s summary of that conversation, see the memorandum, infra.
